headline: Why we’re going wobbly for jelly moulds
summary: Tablescaping, Instagram and modern banquets are feeding the frenzy for these shapely utensils
body_text:
“The world is a better place with more jelly in it,” says chef Skye McAlpine, who has an array of glimmering copper jelly moulds hanging on her kitchen walls. Leaping fish, cherries and a cockerel are just some of the 40 or so shapes she has picked up over the past decade – but most prized of all is a cat: “That, I’m obsessed with. I make a black-velvet jelly with Guinness and then add the nose, tail and paws in panna cotta so that it has white tips. It’s irresistible!” she says.

McAlpine is continuing a tradition that stretches back centuries. Once considered a status symbol and a delicacy, jelly was served at medieval and Tudor banquets – think of Hilary Mantel’s description of towering castles with “edible archers peeping from the battlements” in her novel Bring up the Bodies. Sadly few moulds from this time survive, but Georgian-era Wedgwood core moulds are also highly sought after.
